questions.question: Explain the purpose of waitpid() system call and list its three parameters.
keyPoints: waitpid() allows parent process to wait for specific child termination
pid: specifies which child to wait for (-1 for any, >0 for specific, 0 for process group, <-1 for group ID)
status: pointer to store termination information (or NULL)
options: flags like WNOHANG (non-blocking), WUNTRACED, WCONTINUED

questions.question: What is a zombie process? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Zombie is a terminated process whose entry remains in process table
Created when parent doesn't call wait()/waitpid() to read exit status
Prevention: parent must call wait()/waitpid() to reap child
Alternatively: parent can ignore child death (signal SIGCHLD ignored) or terminate first

questions.question: Differentiate between wait() and waitpid() system calls.
keyPoints: wait() waits for any single child to terminate
waitpid() can wait for a specific child by PID
waitpid() offers non-blocking option via WNOHANG
waitpid() can wait for process group members
wait() is simpler but less flexible

questions.question: Explain the various values that can be passed as the first parameter (pid) to waitpid().
keyPoints: pid > 0: wait for child with that specific PID
pid = 0: wait for any child in same process group
pid = -1: wait for any child (like wait())
pid < -1: wait for any child whose process group ID equals |pid|

questions.question: Write a C code snippet demonstrating the use of waitpid() with WNOHANG to reap multiple terminated children.
keyPoints: Fork child processes in a loop
Use waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG) in a loop
Check return value: >0 means child reaped, 0 means no terminated children, -1 means error
Use WIFEXITED and WEXITSTATUS to get exit codes
Loop continues until all children terminated (ECHILD error)

questions.question: Explain the WIFEXITED and WEXITSTATUS macros with examples.
keyPoints: WIFEXITED(status): returns true if child terminated normally via exit() or return
WEXITSTATUS(status): extracts 8-bit exit code (0-255)
Example: if(WIFEXITED(status)) { int code = WEXITSTATUS(status); }
Always check WIFEXITED before using WEXITSTATUS
These are defined in <sys/wait.h>

questions.question: What are the different options available in waitpid()? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: WNOHANG: return immediately if no child has terminated (non-blocking)
WUNTRACED: report status of stopped child processes (for job control)
WCONTINUED: report status of resumed child processes
Options can be combined using bitwise OR (|)
These allow flexible process status monitoring

questions.question: Explain the complete process of process termination and waiting in Unix. Include zombie process formation and how waitpid() helps in proper resource cleanup.
keyPoints: Child calls exit() or returns from main()
Kernel stores exit status and signal info in process table
Child state changes to zombie - still in table but not running
Parent calls waitpid() to read status
Kernel clears child entry and releases resources
If parent doesn't wait - zombie persists until parent exits
Proper waiting prevents resource leaks
waitpid() provides more control than wait()

questions.question: Write a C program that creates 5 child processes. Each child should exit with a unique exit code. The parent should use waitpid() to reap children in order of their termination and display each child's exit code.
keyPoints: Include <sys/wait.h> and <unistd.h>
Fork 5 children in loop with unique exit codes (i from 1 to 5)
Parent stores child PIDs in array
Use waitpid() with WNOHANG in loop to check termination
Use WIFEXITED to check normal termination
Use WEXITSTATUS to extract exit code
Print child PID and exit code
Handle ECHILD when no children left
Proper error handling

questions.question: Explain the various status macros provided in <sys/wait.h> header file. Give examples of when each would be used.
keyPoints: WIFEXITED: normal termination check
WEXITSTATUS: get exit code (after WIFEXITED)
WIFSIGNALED: killed by signal check
WTERMSIG: get signal number (after WIFSIGNALED)
WIFSTOPPED: stopped process check (job control)
WSTOPSIG: get stop signal (after WIFSTOPPED)
WIFCONTINUED: resumed process check
Example scenarios: normal exit, signal death, stopped/continued processes
